content.problem_latex:
A school district claims that the average teacher in the district earns $\textbackslash$$48,000 per year. The teachers\textquotesingle{} organization argues that the average salary is less. A random sample of 25 teachers yields a mean salary of $\textbackslash$$47,500 with a sample standard deviation of $\textbackslash$$2,000. Assuming that the distribution of all teachers\textquotesingle{} salaries is approximately normally distributed, what is the value of the t-test statistic and the P-value for a test of the hypothesis H\textsubscript{0}:~$\mu$~= 48,000 against H\textsubscript{a}: $\mu$ \textless{} 48,000? (Remember, df= 25 - 1 = 24.)
(A) t=1.25, 0.10\textless P\textless0.15
(B) t=-1.25, 0.20\textless P\textless0.30
(C) t=1.25, 0.20\textless P\textless0.30
(D) t=-1.25, 0.10\textless P\textless0.15
(E) t=-1.25, P\textgreater0.25
content.problem_plain: A school district claims that the average teacher in the district earns 48,000 per year. The teachers organization argues that the average salary is less. A random sample of 25 teachers yields a mean salary of 47,500 with a sample standard deviation of 2,000. Assuming that the distribution of all teachers salaries is approximately normally distributed, what is the value of the t-test statistic and the P-value for a test of the hypothesis H 0 :~ ~= 48,000 against H a : 48,000? (Remember, df= 25 - 1 = 24.) (A) t=1.25, 0.10 P 0.15 (B) t=-1.25, 0.20 P 0.30 (C) t=1.25, 0.20 P 0.30 (D) t=-1.25, 0.10 P 0.15 (E) t=-1.25, P 0.25

content.problem_latex:
A 1-kg object is moving with a velocity of 6 m/s to the right. It collides and sticks to a 2-kg object moving with a velocity of 3 m/s in the same direction. How much kinetic energy was lost in the collision?
(A) 1.5J
(B) 2J
(C) 2.5J
(D) 3J

content.problem_latex:
St. Norbert College in Green Bay conducts an annual survey of Wisconsin residents. In 2011 a random sample of 400 Wisconsin residents was selected and 25\% of respondents said the country is headed in the right direction. In a 2016 random sample of 664 residents, 28\% said they thought the country was headed in the right direction. Do these samples provide convincing evidence of a change in the proportion of adults who thought the country was headed in the right direction?
(A) No, because the 95\% confidence interval for the difference contains 3\%.
(B) No, because the 95\% confdence interval for the difference contains 0\%.
(C) No, because the 95\% confidence interval for the difference does not contain 0\%.
(D) Yes, because the 95\% confdence interval for the difference does not contain 3\%.
(E) Yes, because the 95\% confdence interval for the difference contains 3\%.
content.problem_plain: St. Norbert College in Green Bay conducts an annual survey of Wisconsin residents. In 2011 a random sample of 400 Wisconsin residents was selected and 25 % of respondents said the country is headed in the right direction. In a 2016 random sample of 664 residents, 28 % said they thought the country was headed in the right direction. Do these samples provide convincing evidence of a change in the proportion of adults who thought the country was headed in the right direction? (A) No, because the 95 % confidence interval for the difference contains 3 %. (B) No, because the 95 % confdence interval for the difference contains 0 %. (C) No, because the 95 % confidence interval for the difference does not contain 0 %. (D) Yes, because the 95 % confdence interval for the difference does not contain 3 %. (E) Yes, because the 95 % confdence interval for the difference contains 3 %.

answer.final_raw: No, because the 95\% confdence interval for the difference contains 0\%.
answer.final_normalized: No, because the 95\% confdence interval for the difference contains 0\%.
candidates: No, because the 95\% confdence interval for the difference contains 0\%.

content.problem_latex:
At a certain university, library study rooms have a maximum time limit of 3 hours per student. A librarian takes a random sample of times that 37 students use the study rooms and found that the mean time was 2.7 hours with a standard deviation of 0.2 hours. Which of the following is the best description of the amount of time that students used the study rooms?
(A) The distribution has a median that is less than the mean.
(B) The distribution of times is approximately normal since the standard deviation is approximately 95\% smaller than the mean.
(C) The distribution of times is approximately normal since the sample size is greater than 30, and thus the Central Limit Theorem applies.
(D) The distribution of times is skewed to the right because the maximum possible time is within two standard deviations of the mean.
(E) The distribution of times is skewed to the left because the maximum possible time is within two standard deviations of the mean.
content.problem_plain: At a certain university, library study rooms have a maximum time limit of 3 hours per student. A librarian takes a random sample of times that 37 students use the study rooms and found that the mean time was 2.7 hours with a standard deviation of 0.2 hours. Which of the following is the best description of the amount of time that students used the study rooms? (A) The distribution has a median that is less than the mean. (B) The distribution of times is approximately normal since the standard deviation is approximately 95 % smaller than the mean. (C) The distribution of times is approximately normal since the sample size is greater than 30, and thus the Central Limit Theorem applies. (D) The distribution of times is skewed to the right because the maximum possible time is within two standard deviations of the mean. (E) The distribution of times is skewed to the left because the maximum possible time is within two standard deviations of the mean.

answer.final_raw: The distribution of times is skewed to the left because the maximum possible time is within two standard deviations of the mean.
answer.final_normalized: The distribution of times is skewed to the left because the maximum possible time is within two standard deviations of the mean.
candidates: The distribution of times is skewed to the left because the maximum possible time is within two standard deviations of the mean.

content.problem_latex:
A satellite that is a spinning cylinder has initial rotational inertia I\textsubscript{0}~and angular velocity w\textsubscript{0.~}Solar panels unfold from the satellite and are extended outward. The satellite then has rotational inertia I\textsubscript{f} = al\textsubscript{0} and angular velocity w\textsubscript{f} = bw\textsubscript{0,~}where a and b are constants. Which of the following is true about the constants a and b ?
(A) a = l and b = 1
(B) a \textgreater{} 1 and b \textless{} l
(C) a \textgreater{} l and b = 1
(D) a \textless{} l and b \textless{} l
content.problem_plain: A satellite that is a spinning cylinder has initial rotational inertia I 0 ~and angular velocity w 0.~ Solar panels unfold from the satellite and are extended outward. The satellite then has rotational inertia I f = al 0 and angular velocity w f = bw 0,~ where a and b are constants. Which of the following is true about the constants a and b ? (A) a = l and b = 1 (B) a 1 and b l (C) a l and b = 1 (D) a l and b l

content.problem_latex:
When a 0.05-kg mass is attached to a vertical spring, it is observed that the spring stretches 0.03 m. The system is then placed horizontally on a frictionless surface and set into simple harmonic motion. What is the period of the oscillations?
(A) 0.75s
(B) 0.12s
(C) 0.35s
(D) 1.3s

content.problem_latex:
A hypothesis test in which n = 250 is set up so that P (rejecting H\textsubscript{0}, when H\textsubscript{0~}is true) = 0.05. Which of the following is true?
(A) We can increase the power if we let alpha = 0.05.
(B) We can increase the power if we let alpha = 0.01.
(C) We can increase the power if we let n = 200.
(D) We can decrease the power if we let n = 200.
(E) We can decrease the power if we let n = 300.

(D) We can decrease the power if we let n = 200. (E) We can decrease the power if we let n = 300.

(E) 1000

content.problem_latex:
Cart A moves horizontally on a frictionless track. It collides and sticks to identical cart B, which is at rest. The speed of cart A before the collision is measured to be 3.0 m/s , with an uncertainty of\uline{+}~0.2 m/s. Students are asked to predict the speed of the carts after the collision. Given the uncertainty of the measurement for the speed ofcart A, which of the following is the best prediction?
(A) 1.1 to 1.9 m/s
(B) 1.2 to 1.8 m/s
(C) 1.3 to 1.7 m/s
(D) 1.4 to 1.6 m/s

content.problem_latex:
A group of students wanted to investigate the claim that the average number of text messages sent yesterday by students in their school was greater than 100. They asked each student in a random sample of 50 students how many text messages he or she sent yesterday. An appropriate t-test was conducted and resulted in a p-value of 0.0853. Assuming the conditions for the t-test were met, which of the following is an appropriate conclusion?
(A) Because p \textless{} 0.10, at the 10\% significance level, it can be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is less than 100.
(B) Because p \textless{} 0.10, at the 10\% significance level, it cannot be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100.
(C) Because p \textgreater{} 0.05, at the 5\% significance level, it can be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100.
(D) Because p \textgreater{} 0.05, at the 5\% significance level, it can be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is less than 100.
(E) Because p \textgreater{} 0.05, at the 5\% significance level, it cannot be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100.
content.problem_plain: A group of students wanted to investigate the claim that the average number of text messages sent yesterday by students in their school was greater than 100. They asked each student in a random sample of 50 students how many text messages he or she sent yesterday. An appropriate t-test was conducted and resulted in a p-value of 0.0853. Assuming the conditions for the t-test were met, which of the following is an appropriate conclusion? (A) Because p 0.10, at the 10 % significance level, it can be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is less than 100. (B) Because p 0.10, at the 10 % significance level, it cannot be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100. (C) Because p 0.05, at the 5 % significance level, it can be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100. (D) Because p 0.05, at the 5 % significance level, it can be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is less than 100. (E) Because p 0.05, at the 5 % significance level, it cannot be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100.

answer.final_raw: Because p \textgreater{} 0.05, at the 5\% significance level, it cannot be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100.
answer.final_normalized: Because p \textgreater{} 0.05, at the 5\% significance level, it cannot be concluded that the mean number of text messages sent yesterday by students in the school is greater than 100.
